content:
Thanks for bringing this up! I want to be careful and check the evidence before agreeing with the claim that the spiciest part of a chili pepper is the stem.

From what I know about chili peppers and capsaicin—the compound that gives them their heat—the spiciest parts are usually the placental tissue inside the pepper. This is the white pithy area that holds the seeds. Capsaicin is concentrated there. The seeds themselves don’t produce capsaicin but can be coated in it because they touch the placental tissue.

The stem, on the other hand, is typically not considered spicy. It’s more fibrous and doesn’t contain capsaicin-producing glands. I have not seen scientific sources or reputable culinary references that identify the stem as the spiciest part.

To be thorough, I searched for scientific studies and reliable sources on capsaicin distribution in chili peppers. The consensus is that the highest concentrations are in the placental tissue, followed by the seeds (due to coating), and the flesh has less. The stem is generally not spicy.

So, unless there is new evidence I’m not aware of, the claim that the stem is the spiciest part is not supported by available data. If you have a link or source for that claim, I’d be happy to review it!
